title: Titus 3
subtitle: Saved by Grace, Devoted to Good Works

sections.header: Verses 1 8 — "He Saved Us Through the Washing of Rebirth"

heb.paragraph: The noun παλιγγενεσία (palingenesia) appears only twice in the NT: here and in Matt 19:28 (where it refers to the cosmic renewal at Christ's return). The word combines πάλιν (again) and γένεσις (birth/origin), yielding 'rebirth' or 'regeneration.' In Stoic philosophy it described the periodic renewal of the cosmos. Paul applies it to individual spiritual renewal through the Holy Spirit—a usage that aligns with the Johannine 'born again/from above' language (John 3:3–5).

Paired with παλιγγενεσία, the noun ἀνακαίνωσις (anakainōsis, renewal) appears also in Rom 12:2 ('renewal of the mind'). Together, the two nouns describe salvation as both a decisive new beginning (rebirth) and an ongoing transformation (renewal). The Holy Spirit is the agent of both, poured out generously (πλουσίως, richly) through Christ.

refs.note: Jesus' teaching on being 'born again/from above' through water and the Spirit provides the closest conceptual parallel to the 'washing of rebirth' language here.
Paul's call to be 'transformed by the renewing of your mind' uses the same ἀνακαίνωσις (renewal) vocabulary.
The before/after structure (dead in sins → made alive by grace, not works → created for good works) is the closest structural parallel in the Pauline corpus.
The promise of the Spirit's outpouring, fulfilled at Pentecost, underlies the language of the Spirit 'poured out generously' through Jesus Christ.

notes.note: Remind the people to be subject to rulers and authorities, to be obedient, to be ready to do whatever is good, to slander no one, to be peaceable and considerate.
At one time we too were foolish, disobedient, deceived, and enslaved by all kinds of passions and pleasures. We lived in malice and envy, being hated and hating one another.

But when the kindness and love of God our Savior appeared, he saved us—not because of righteous things we had done, but because of his mercy. He saved us through the washing of rebirth and renewal by the Holy Spirit, poured out generously through Jesus Christ, so that, justified by his grace, we might become heirs having the hope of eternal life.

This is a trustworthy saying, and I want you to stress these things so that those who have trusted in God may be careful to devote themselves to doing what is good. These things are excellent and profitable for everyone.

Paul grounds civic obedience and social virtues in the memory of what believers once were and the grace that transformed them. We have no room for arrogance toward pagans—we were once exactly like them. Salvation came not through our merit but through God's mercy, applied through the washing of regeneration and the Holy Spirit's renewal. This comprehensive salvation—justification, regeneration, and inheritance—is entirely God's work. The appropriate response is devotion to good works—not to earn salvation but to express it.

The phrase 'washing of rebirth' (λουτροῦ παλιγγενεσίας) likely refers to baptism as the outward sign of the inward reality of spiritual regeneration. The genitive is probably epexegetical: the 'washing that consists of rebirth.'

This is the fifth and final 'trustworthy saying' in the Pastoral Epistles. Some scholars debate whether it refers forward (to the devotion-to-good-works instruction) or backward (to the salvation summary of vv. 4–7).

The civic instruction (vv. 1–2) and the before-conversion catalogue (v. 3) serve a joint function: they remind the community that their social obligations are grounded not in civic virtue per se but in gratitude for transformation. The 'we ourselves were' (ῆμεν γάρ ποτε καὶ ἡμεῖς) includes Paul himself, eliminating any posture of superiority.

The soteriological creed is the Pastorals' most explicit trinitarian passage: God the Father saves through the Spirit's work, applied through Christ. The λουτρόν (washing) almost certainly refers to baptism. The fifth πιστός λόγος transitions from theology to ethics: those who have experienced this salvation must devote themselves (προϊστασθαι) to good works—the Pastorals' irreducible ethical criterion.

The passage deploys the classic before/after conversion pattern (cf. Eph 2:1–10; Col 3:5–17) to ground social ethics in soteriology. The pre-conversion catalogue (v. 3) serves an equalizing function: we were no different from the pagans among whom we live, so condescension is ruled out. The baptismal creed (vv. 4–7) is the letter's second great soteriological statement (after 2:11–14), now emphasizing the Spirit's role in regeneration and renewal. The trinitarian structure—Father saves, Spirit renews, Son mediates—is the fullest trinitarian formula in the Pastorals. The fifth trustworthy saying (v. 8) drives home the practical conclusion: salvation by mercy produces devotion to good works.

hist.context: Titus 3:3–7 is widely regarded as a pre-Pauline baptismal creed or hymn, parallel in structure and function to 2 Tim 1:9–10 and Tit 2:11–14. It follows a before/after pattern: 'we ourselves were' (v. 3, catalogue of pre-conversion vices) followed by 'but when the kindness and love of God our Savior appeared' (vv. 4–7, salvation by mercy, not works). The emphasis on the Holy Spirit's role in regeneration makes this the most explicitly trinitarian passage in the Pastorals. The fifth trustworthy saying (v. 8) introduces the letter's final ethical principle: believers must be 'careful to devote themselves to doing what is good.'

heb.paragraph: The adjective αἱρετικός (hairetikos) is a NT hapax from which English derives 'heretic.' In this context it does not yet carry the later technical meaning of one who holds false doctrine; rather, it denotes a person who causes factions or divisions (αἵρεσις, faction/party). The two-warning procedure before rejection echoes Jesus' teaching in Matt 18:15–17 and establishes a graduated disciplinary process.

refs.note: Jesus' teaching on church discipline prescribes a graduated process of private warning before public action—the same principle underlying the two-warning procedure here.
Paul's warning to avoid those who cause divisions and create obstacles parallels the instruction to reject a divisive person after two warnings.
Paul's instruction not to associate with an unrepentant church member provides the broader Pauline context for the discipline described here.

notes.note: Avoid foolish controversies, genealogies, arguments, and quarrels about the law, because these are unprofitable and useless. Warn a divisive person once, then warn them a second time. After that, have nothing to do with them. Such people are warped and sinful; they are self-condemned.

Paul will send Artemas or Tychicus; Titus should come to Nicopolis for winter. Zenas the lawyer and Apollos should be sent on their way with everything they need. Our people must learn to devote themselves to doing what is good, in order to provide for urgent needs and not live unproductive lives.
Everyone with Paul sends greetings. Greet those who love us in the faith. Grace be with you all.

Paul's closing instructions draw a sharp line between profitable and unprofitable activity. Foolish controversies about genealogies and the law are a waste of time; divisive persons must be warned and then excluded if they persist. The positive alternative is devotion to good works—the practical fruit that marks genuine faith. The letter ends as it began: with grace.
The term αἱρετικόν here means 'factious, divisive' rather than the later technical sense of 'heretical.' The two-warning discipline procedure is graduated and proportional.
Zenas 'the lawyer' (τὸν νομικόν) may refer to expertise in Jewish law (like the 'lawyers' of the Gospels) or Roman law. The title is otherwise unattested for a Christian leader.

The αἱρετικός ἄνθρωπος (factious person) is described as ἐξέστραπται (perverted/warped)—a perfect passive indicating a condition produced by persistent rejection of truth. The two-warning limit is merciful but firm: pastoral patience has boundaries, and prolonged engagement with the factious becomes counterproductive.

The personal notices (Artemas, Tychicus, Zenas, Apollos) locate the letter within a plausible network of Pauline co-workers. The closing ethical charge—προϊστασθαι καλῶν ἔργων (to devote themselves to good works)—is the letter's final word on authentic faith: it produces visible, practical, community-serving action.

The closing section balances negative (avoid controversies, reject the factious) and positive (devote to good works, support itinerant workers) instructions. The αἱρετικός instruction provides a graduated discipline procedure that respects due process while protecting the community from extended disruption. The final charge to good works creates a frame with v. 8, enclosing the letter's ending in the Pastorals' most characteristic ethical imperative. The benediction—'grace be with you all'—shifts from the singular Titus to the plural congregation, confirming the letter's dual audience.

hist.context: The closing section of Titus combines a final anti-heresy warning (vv. 9–11), travel instructions (vv. 12–13), a general ethical principle (v. 14), and a benediction (v. 15). The reference to wintering at Nicopolis (v. 12) provides a geographical anchor: there were several cities named Nicopolis, but the most likely candidate is the one in Epirus (western Greece), founded by Augustus to commemorate his victory at Actium. The concluding charge—'our people must learn to devote themselves to doing what is good'—echoes v. 8 and forms an inclusio for the letter's practical instruction.

themes.note: Mercy dominates the baptismal creed (saved not by works but by mercy). Faith and Covenant frame the before/after conversion pattern. Mission and Justice drive the closing devotion-to-good-works charge.

rows.label: vv.3–7
rows.text: Baptismal creed: from vice to grace

rows.label: v.8
rows.text: Fifth trustworthy saying: devotion to good works

lit.note: Chapter 3 concludes Titus with its second great soteriological passage (vv. 3–7), the final trustworthy saying (v. 8), and closing instructions (vv. 9–15). The before/after conversion pattern grounds both civic virtue and good works in the experience of divine mercy.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Titus
ppl.text: Titus 3:3–7 is widely regarded as a pre-Pauline baptismal creed or hymn, parallel in structure and function to 2 Tim 1:9–10 and Tit 2:11–14…

ppl.name: Artemas
ppl.text: Artemas is a key figure in Saved by Grace, Devoted to Good Works.

ppl.name: Tychicus
ppl.text: Tychicus is a key figure in Saved by Grace, Devoted to Good Works.

ppl.name: Zenas
ppl.text: Zenas the lawyer and Apollos should be sent on their way with everything they need…

ppl.name: Apollos
ppl.role: Eloquent Alexandrian teacher; discipled by Priscilla and Aquila
ppl.text: Apollos is an Alexandrian Jew who arrives at Ephesus knowing the way of God accurately but only knowing John’s baptism (Acts 18:24–25). After Priscilla and Aquila complete his instruction, he becomes a powerful apologist in Achaia, "vigorously refuting his Jewish opponents in public debate" (Acts 18:28). Paul’s Corinthian correspondence shows Apollos became an unintended source of factionalism (1 Cor 1:12), though Paul regards him as a fellow worker.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Saved by Grace, Devoted to Good Works within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

discourse.title: Titus 3 — Good Works, Salvation by Mercy, Avoiding Controversy
discourse.thesis: Remind believers to be subject to authorities and ready for good works. We were saved not by works but by God's mercy through rebirth and renewal. Avoid foolish controversies.

nodes.label: Civic and social conduct (3:1-2)
nodes.text: Remind them to be submissive to rulers and authorities, to be obedient, ready for every good work, to speak evil of no one, to avoid quarreling, to be gentle, showing perfect courtesy toward all people.

nodes.label: What we were (3:3)
nodes.text: For we ourselves were once foolish, disobedient, led astray, slaves to various passions and pleasures, passing our days in malice and envy, hated by others and hating one another.

nodes.label: Salvation by mercy (3:4-7)
nodes.text: But when the goodness and loving kindness of God our Savior appeared, he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit, whom he poured out on us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior, so that being justified by his grace we might become heirs according to the hope of eternal life.

nodes.label: Trustworthy saying (3:8)
nodes.text: The saying is trustworthy, and I want you to insist on these things, so that those who have believed in God may be careful to devote themselves to good works. These things are excellent and profitable for people.

nodes.label: Avoid controversies (3:9-11)
nodes.text: But avoid foolish controversies, genealogies, dissensions, and quarrels about the law, for they are unprofitable and worthless. As for a person who stirs up division, after warning him once and then twice, have nothing more to do with him, knowing that such a person is warped and sinful; he is self-condemned.

nodes.label: Personal instructions (3:12-15)
nodes.text: Paul asks Titus to come to him at Nicopolis. Speed Zenas and Apollos on their way. Let our people learn to devote themselves to good works for pressing needs. Greet those who love us in the faith. Grace be with you all.

discourse.note: Titus 3:4-7 is often called a 'faithful saying' — a compact creedal formula summarizing salvation by grace through regeneration.

coaching.tip: 'He saved us, not because of righteous things we had done, but because of his mercy' (v. 5). Salvation's cause: divine mercy, not human merit. 'Washing of rebirth and renewal by the Holy Spirit' (v. 5)—baptismal imagery pointing to regeneration's reality.

'Warn a divisive person once, and then warn them a second time. After that, have nothing to do with them' (v. 10). Church discipline follows clear process: two warnings, then separation. Unity matters enough to enforce boundaries. Patience has limits.
